figures.caption: Table 1. Quantitative comparisons on the LOL [68], LSRW [18], and MIT5K [3] datasets for LLIE. The best results are highlighted in bold and the second-best are in underlined. ‘SL’ and ‘UL’ denote the methods as supervised and unsupervised, respectively.
Table 2. Quantitative comparisons on the BAID [49] and Backlit300 [42] datasets for BIE. The best results are highlighted in bold and the second-best are in underlined.

Table 3. Quantitative comparisons on the MSEC [1] and SICE [4] datasets for MEC. The best results are highlighted in bold and the second-best are in underlined. ‘SL’ and ‘UL’ denote the methods as supervised and unsupervised, respectively.

Figure 4. Qualitative comparison on the LSRW [18] (row 1), BAID [49] (row 2), and SICE [4] (row 3) test sets for low-light image enhancement (LLIE), backlit image enhancement (BIE), and multiple exposure correction (MEC), respectively.

Figure 2. The overall pipeline of our proposed ZeroIDIR framework. We first perform Retinex decomposition on the illumination degradation image Id to generate a reflectance map Rd and an illumination map Ld. Then, we design an adaptive gamma correction module (AGCM) to achieve spatially adaptive illumination correction on the Ld through two dedicated branches to prepare an illuminationcorrected only image I ′ d for subsequent reconstruction. The refined image is then treated as the intermediate perturbed noisy state xt∗ and fed into a perturbed consistency diffusion model (PCDM) for reconstruction, with the guidance of the proposed perturbed diffusion consistency loss Lpdc toward the final restored sharp image Îd in a zero-reference manner.
